QUESTION:
A 54-year-old G2P2 presents to her gynecologist's office with complaints of frequent hot flashes, malaise, insomnia, and mild mood swings for 2 weeks. She has also noticed some pain with intercourse and vaginal dryness during this time. She is otherwise healthy besides hyperlipidemia, controlled on atorvastatin. She has no other past medical history, but underwent hysterectomy for postpartum hemorrhage. She is desiring of a medication to control her symptoms. Which of the following is the most appropriate short-term medical therapy in this patient for symptomatic relief?

CHOICES:
  A. Hormonal replacement therapy with estrogen alone
  B. Hormonal replacement therapy with combined estrogen/progesterone
  C. Paroxetine
  D. Gabapentin

CONTEXT:
(no context)

Produce the JSON object.

response:
{
  "case_facts": [
    "54-year-old woman with menopausal symptoms",
    "history of hysterectomy (postpartum hemorrhage)",
    "hyperlipidemia on atorvastatin",
    "symptoms: hot flashes, malaise, insomnia, mood swings, dyspareunia, vaginal dryness",
    "desires medication for short-term symptomatic relief"
  ],
  "task_type": "therapy_selection",
  "decision_factors": [
    "presence or absence of uterus (hysterectomy eliminates need for progestin)",
    "cardiovascular and thromboembolic risk (including controlled hyperlipidemia)",
    "patient preference for hormonal vs non-hormonal therapy",
    "short-term symptom relief goal",
    "contraindications to estrogen or non-hormonal agents"
  ],
  "knowledge_slots": [
    "Estrogen alone is sufficient for vasomotor symptoms in women without a uterus",
    "Combined estrogen-progesterone is indicated only if uterus is present",
    "Paroxetine (SSRI) is FDA-approved for moderate-to-severe vasomotor symptoms",
    "Gabapentin can reduce hot flash frequency and severity, off-label use",
    "Statins do not contraindicate hormone therapy but require monitoring of lipids"
  ],
  "candidate_considerations": [
    {
      "choice_key": "A",
      "relevant_if": ["patient has no uterus", "no contraindications to estrogen", "prefers hormonal therapy"],
      "less_relevant_if": ["patient has active estrogen-sensitive cancer", "history of thromboembolism", "prefers non-hormonal options"]
    },
    {
      "choice_key": "B",
      "relevant_if": ["patient has an intact uterus", "requires progesterone protection"],
      "less_relevant_if": ["patient had hysterectomy (no uterus)", "unnecessary additional hormone exposure"]
    },
    {
      "choice_key": "C",
      "relevant_if": ["patient prefers non-hormonal therapy", "has contraindications to estrogen", "desires short-term symptom control"],
      "less_relevant_if": ["patient already on SSRI/SNRI", "contraindications to paroxetine (e.g., MAOI use)", "prefers hormonal therapy with low risk"]
    },
    {
      "choice_key": "D",
      "relevant_if": ["patient prefers non-hormonal therapy", "estrogen contraindicated and SSRI not tolerated", "needs alternative for hot flashes"],
      "less_relevant_if": ["patient has dizziness or sedation risk", "prefers hormonal therapy", "gabapentin not effective for mood symptoms"]
    }
  ],
  "missing_information": [
    "personal or family history of breast cancer or thromboembolic disease",
    "patient's preference for hormonal vs non-hormonal treatment",
    "severity and frequency of symptoms",
    "contraindications to SSRIs or gabapentin"
  ],
  "format_confidence": 0.95
}

His temperature is 99°F (37°C), blood pressure is 92/64 mmHg, pulse is 98/min, respirations are 14/min, and SpO2 is 98%. A physical examination demonstrates an unresponsive patient with extensive burn injuries throughout his torso and lower extremities.

Hemoglobin: 13 g/dL
Hematocrit: 36%
Leukocyte count: 10,670/mm^3 with normal differential
Platelet count: 180,000/mm^3

Na+: 135 mEq/L
Cl-: 98 mEq/L
K+: 4.7 mEq/L

Triiodothyronine: 48 ng/dL
Thyroxine: 10 ug/dL
Ca2+:  8.7 mg/dL
AST: 89 U/L
ALT: 135 U/L

What is the best course of management for this patient?

CHOICES:
  A. Continued management of his burn wounds
  B. Immediate administration of propanolol
  C. Regular levothyroxine sodium injections
  D. Start patient on intravenous ceftriaxone and vancomycin

QUESTION:
A 23-year old man is brought to the emergency department by his brother after trying to jump out of a moving car. He says that the Federal Bureau of Investigation has been following him for the last 7 months. He recently quit his job at a local consulting firm to work on his mission to rid the world from evil. His temperature is 36.7°C (98.1°F), pulse is 90/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 120/86 mm Hg. On mental status examination, his response to the first question lasted 5 minutes without interruption. He switched topics a number of times and his speech was pressured. He spoke excessively about his plan to “bring absolute justice to the world”. He has a labile affect. There is no evidence of suicidal ideation. A toxicology screen is negative. He is admitted to the hospital for his symptoms and starts therapy. One week later, he develops difficulty walking and a tremor that improves with activity. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's latest symptoms?

CHOICES:
  A. Dopamine antagonist
  B. Serotonin–norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor
  C. Histamine antagonist
  D. Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor

QUESTION:
A 52-year-old man presents for a routine checkup. Past medical history is remarkable for stage 1 systemic hypertension and hepatitis A infection diagnosed 10 years ago. He takes aspirin, rosuvastatin, enalapril daily, and a magnesium supplement every once in a while. He is planning to visit Ecuador for a week-long vacation and is concerned about malaria prophylaxis before his travel. The physician advised taking 1 primaquine pill every day while he is there and for 7 consecutive days after leaving Ecuador. On the third day of his trip, the patient develops an acute onset headache, dizziness, shortness of breath, and fingertips and toes turning blue. His blood pressure is 135/80 mm Hg, heart rate is 94/min, respiratory rate is 22/min, temperature is 36.9℃ (98.4℉), and blood oxygen saturation is 97% in room air. While drawing blood for his laboratory workup, the nurse notes that his blood has a chocolate brown color. Which of the following statements best describes the etiology of this patient’s most likely condition?

CHOICES:
  A. The patient’s condition is due to consumption of water polluted with nitrates.
  B. This condition resulted from primaquine overdose.
  C. The condition developed because of his concomitant use of primaquine and magnesium supplement.
  D. It is a type B adverse drug reaction.

CONTEXT:
(no context)

Produce the JSON object.

response:
{
  "case_facts": [
    "52-year-old man on aspirin, rosuvastatin, enalapril, magnesium supplement",
    "Started primaquine for malaria prophylaxis before Ecuador trip",
    "On third day of trip developed headache, dizziness, dyspnea, cyanosis of digits",
    "Blood drawn had chocolate brown color",
    "Vitals stable except mild tachypnea; SpO2 97% on room air"
  ],
  "task_type": "adverse_effect",
  "decision_factors": [
    "Temporal association between primaquine initiation and symptom onset",
    "Known adverse effect profile of primaquine (methemoglobinemia)",
    "Potential interaction with other medications or supplements",
    "Alternative causes (e.g., nitrate ingestion from water)",
    "Classification of adverse drug reaction (type A dose-related vs type B idiosyncratic)"
  ],
  "knowledge_slots": [
    "Primaquine can cause methemoglobinemia via oxidative stress",
    "Methemoglobinemia presents with chocolate brown blood, cyanosis, dyspnea",
    "Type A reactions are dose-dependent; type B are idiosyncratic and unpredictable",
    "Nitrates (e.g., in water) can cause acquired methemoglobinemia",
    "Magnesium supplement does not interact with primaquine to cause methemoglobinemia"
  ],
  "candidate_considerations": [
    {
      "choice_key": "A",
      "relevant_if": ["Patient ingested nitrates from contaminated water during travel"],
      "less_relevant_if": ["Symptoms onset soon after starting primaquine rather than prolonged exposure; no reported water source issue"]
    },
    {
      "choice_key": "B",
      "relevant_if": ["Primaquine dose was supratherapeutic or patient had underlying susceptibility"],
      "less_relevant_if": ["Prophylactic primaquine dose is typically low; overdose unlikely without evidence"]
    },
    {
      "choice_key": "C",
      "relevant_if": ["Magnesium supplement interacts with primaquine to increase oxidant stress"],
      "less_relevant_if": ["No known pharmacokinetic or pharmacodynamic interaction between magnesium and primaquine"]
    },
    {
      "choice_key": "D",
      "relevant_if": ["Reaction is idiosyncratic (unpredictable, not dose-dependent)"],
      "less_relevant_if": ["Methemoglobinemia from primaquine is often dose-related (type A) though may be variable"]
    }
  ],
  "missing_information": [
    "Patient's G6PD status",
    "Exact dose and duration of primaquine",
    "History of methemoglobinemia or nitrate exposure",
    "Baseline methemoglobin level"
  ],
  "format_confidence": 0.85
}

  D. Hemolytic transfusion reaction

QUESTION:
A 20-year-old woman presents with menorrhagia for the past several years. She says that her menses “have always been heavy”, and she has experienced easy bruising for as long as she can remember. Family history is significant for her mother, who had similar problems with bruising easily. The patient's vital signs include: heart rate 98/min, respiratory rate 14/min, temperature 36.1°C (96.9°F), and blood pressure 110/87 mm Hg. Physical examination is unremarkable. Laboratory tests show the following: platelet count 200,000/mm3, PT 12 seconds, and PTT 43 seconds. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient’s symptoms?

CHOICES:
  A. Hemophilia A
  B. Lupus anticoagulant
  C. Protein C deficiency
  D. Von Willebrand disease

CONTEXT:
(no context)

response:
{
  "case_facts": [
    "20-year-old woman with lifelong menorrhagia and easy bruising",
    "Family history: mother with similar bruising",
    "Vitals: HR 98, RR 14, temp 36.1°C, BP 110/87",
    "Physical exam unremarkable",
    "Platelet count: 200,000/mm3 (normal)",
    "PT: 12 seconds (normal)",
    "PTT: 43 seconds (prolonged)"
  ],
  "task_type": "diagnosis",
  "decision_factors": [
    "Isolated prolonged PTT with normal PT and platelet count",
    "Lifelong bleeding history suggesting inherited disorder",
    "Family history in mother consistent with autosomal dominant or X-linked inheritance",
    "Clinical presentation includes menorrhagia and easy bruising"
  ],
  "knowledge_slots": [
    "Hemophilia A: X-linked recessive, prolonged PTT, normal PT/platelets; usually affects males; rare in females unless skewed X-inactivation or Turner syndrome",
    "Lupus anticoagulant: prolonged PTT due to inhibitor, associated with thrombosis, not bleeding; mixing study does not correct",
    "Protein C deficiency: autosomal dominant, usually presents with thrombosis; PT/PTT typically normal",
    "Von Willebrand disease: most common inherited bleeding disorder, autosomal dominant; causes mucocutaneous bleeding, menorrhagia; PTT may be prolonged due to low factor VIII; platelet count normal"
  ],
  "candidate_considerations": [
    {
      "choice_key": "A",
      "relevant_if": [
        "Patient is female but could have skewed X-inactivation or Turner syndrome",
        "Family history is X-linked recessive pattern"
      ],
      "less_relevant_if": [
        "Female patient with mother affected (typical hemophilia A would require affected father or carrier mother with affected son)",
        "Severe factor VIII deficiency unlikely in female without specific genetic scenario"
      ]
    },
    {
      "choice_key": "B",
      "relevant_if": [
        "Prolonged PTT does not correct with mixing study",
        "Patient has history of thrombosis rather than bleeding"
      ],
      "less_relevant_if": [
        "Presentation is bleeding, not thrombosis",
        "Lupus anticoagulant typically causes prolonged PTT but no bleeding diathesis"
      ]
    },
    {
      "choice_key": "C",
      "relevant_if": [
        "Patient has history of thrombosis (e.g., deep vein thrombosis)",
        "Prolonged PTT is not typical; protein C deficiency usually has normal coagulation times"
      ],
      "less_relevant_if": [
        "Normal PT and PTT are expected in protein C deficiency",
        "Bleeding is not the typical presentation (more often thrombosis)"
      ]
    },
    {
      "choice_key": "D",
      "relevant_if": [
        "Autosomal dominant inheritance suspected (mother affected)",
        "Mucocutaneous bleeding symptoms: menorrhagia, easy bruising",
        "Prolonged PTT due to low factor VIII secondary to low von Willebrand factor"
      ],
      "less_relevant_if": [
        "Platelet count is normal (but in type 2B or pseudo VWD might have low platelets)",
        "No other family members affected (though mother is affected here)"
      ]
    }
  ],
  "missing_information": [
    "Mixing study result for PTT (correction vs. no correction)",
    "von Willebrand antigen, activity, and factor VIII levels",
    "Family history details including father and siblings"
  ],
  "format_confidence": 0.95
}

His vital signs include: blood pressure 110/80 mm Hg, pulse rate 100/min, and respirations 10/min and shallow. On physical examination, his extremities are cold and clammy. In half an hour, the patient is fully conscious, alert and responsive. He denies any medical illnesses, hospitalizations, or surgeries in the past. Physical examination reveals injection track marks along both arms. He admits to the use of cocaine and heroin. He smokes cigarettes and consumes alcohol. His vital signs are now stable.   C. Alcohol intoxication
  D. Opioid intoxication

A 54-year-old man comes to the physician because of a painful mass in his left thigh for 3 days. He underwent a left lower limb angiography for femoral artery stenosis and had a stent placed 2 weeks ago. He has peripheral artery disease, coronary artery disease, hypercholesterolemia and type 2 diabetes mellitus. He has smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for 34 years. Current medications include enalapril, aspirin, simvastatin, metformin, and sitagliptin. His temperature is 36.7°C (98°F), pulse is 88/min, and blood pressure is 116/72 mm Hg. Examination shows a 3-cm (1.2-in) tender, pulsatile mass in the left groin. The skin over the area of the mass shows no erythema and is cool to the touch. A loud bruit is heard on auscultation over this area. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Results of a complete blood count and serum electrolyte concentrations show no abnormalities. Duplex ultrasonography shows an echolucent sac connected to the common femoral artery, with pulsatile and turbulent blood flow between the artery and the sac. Which of the following is the most appropriate next best step in management?

CHOICES:
  A. Ultrasound-guided thrombin injection
  B. Coil embolization
  C. Ultrasound-guided compression
  D. Schedule surgical repair

QUESTION:

She denies any recent illnesses, weight change, fever, vaginal bleeding, pain, or medication use. Physical examination demonstrates Tanner stage 4 development. A pelvic ultrasound shows an ovarian mass. Laboratory studies demonstrates an elevated level of estrogen. What is the most likely diagnosis?

CHOICES:
  A. Granulosa cell tumor
  B. Idiopathic precocious puberty
  C. McCune-Albright syndrome
  D. Sertoli-Leydig tumor

He is unable to recall any further details and does not have any chest pain or dizziness. He has a history of hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, gastroparesis, and osteoarthritis of the knees. Medications include lisinopril, metformin, and ondansetron as needed for nausea. He also takes methadone daily for chronic pain. Apart from an abrasion on his forehead, he appears well. His temperature is 37.2 °C (98.9 F), heart rate is 104/min and regular, and blood pressure is 135/70 mm Hg. While he is in the emergency department, he loses consciousness again. Telemetry shows polymorphic ventricular tachycardia with cyclic alteration of the QRS axis that spontaneously resolves after 30 seconds. Results of a complete blood count, serum electrolyte concentrations, and serum thyroid studies show no abnormalities. Cardiac enzymes are within normal limits. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of this patient's syncope?

CHOICES:
  A. Prolonged QT interval
  B. Prinzmetal angina
  C. Brugada syndrome
  D. Hypomagnesemia
"

CONTEXT:
(no context)

Produce the JSON object.

response:
{
  "case_facts": [
    "62-year-old man with syncope and palpitations",
    "Polymorphic ventricular tachycardia with cyclic QRS axis (torsades de pointes)",
    "Normal complete blood count, electrolytes, thyroid studies, cardiac enzymes",
    "Medications: lisinopril, metformin, ondansetron, methadone",
    "History: hypertension, type 2 diabetes, gastroparesis, osteoarthritis"
  ],
  "task_type": "diagnosis",
  "decision_factors": [
    "Association of torsades de pointes with prolonged QT interval",
    "QT-prolonging potential of prescribed medications (ondansetron, methadone)",
    "Normal electrolyte panel excludes hypomagnesemia as immediate cause",
    "ECG findings of Brugada pattern or ST elevation for other diagnoses",
    "Absence of chest pain or known coronary artery disease history"
  ],
  "knowledge_slots": [
    "Torsades de pointes is a polymorphic VT with twisting QRS axis often due to prolonged QT interval",
    "Ondansetron and methadone are associated with QT prolongation",
    "Brugada syndrome presents with ST elevation in V1-V3 and risk of ventricular arrhythmias",
    "Prinzmetal angina causes transient ST elevation due to coronary vasospasm"
  ],
  "candidate_considerations": [
    {
      "choice_key": "A",
      "relevant_if": ["ECG shows prolonged QT interval; patient is on QT-prolonging medications"],
      "less_relevant_if": ["Known family history of short QT; recent electrolyte abnormalities absent"]
    },
    {
      "choice_key": "B",
      "relevant_if": ["ECG shows transient ST elevation during pain; known coronary vasospasm history"],
      "less_relevant_if": ["No chest pain episodes; no ST elevation reported; arrhythmia pattern not typical of ischemia"]
    },
    {
      "choice_key": "C",
      "relevant_if": ["ECG shows coved ST elevation in V1-V3; personal or family history of sudden cardiac death"],
      "less_relevant_if": ["Normal ECG; no ST elevation; arrhythmia is torsades rather than typical Brugada VT"]
    },
    {
      "choice_key": "D",
      "relevant_if": ["Low serum magnesium documented; dietary or absorption issues causing deficit"],
      "less_relevant_if": ["Initial electrolyte panel normal; magnesium level specifically not reported but other normal"]
    }
  ],
  "missing_information": [
    "ECG findings including QT interval and ST segments",
    "Serum magnesium level specifically",
    "Family history of arrhythmias or sudden death"
  ],
  "format_confidence": 0.95
}

(no context)

QUESTION:
A 28-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by a friend after fainting at work and hitting her head. She is conscious, alert, and in pain as she sustained a deep laceration above her right orbit. When asked about prior fainting episodes, she says that she has had them since childhood, but she felt it was "nothing serious". She also says she has frequent palpitations, shortness of breath, nausea, and, at times, chest pain and attributes this to "working too hard." Her pulse is 110/min, respirations are 20/min, temperature is 37.4°C (99.3°F), and blood pressure is 110/78 mm Hg. Physical examination shows tachycardia and mild hypotension. The patient's electrocardiogram is obtained. Which of the following drugs is the preferable choice for first line treatment of the patient's condition?

CHOICES:
  A. Calcium gluconate
  B. Flecainide
  C. Magnesium sulfate
  D. Procainamide

(no context)

QUESTION:
A 54-year-old man is brought to the physician by his wife because of progressive difficulty walking during the past 3 months. He has not been able to walk without assistance for the past month and has started to use a wheelchair. He also reports having urinary incontinence for 1 year. His wife says that he has begun to slur his words and has become very difficult to understand. His temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is 70/min, and respirations are 16/min. His blood pressure is 130/80mm Hg while sitting and 110/65mm Hg when standing. He is oriented to person and place but not to time. Neurological examination shows a mild tremor in his right hand and rigidity in his upper and lower extremities. He is unable to perform repetitive rotary forearm movements. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

CHOICES:
  A. Multiple system atrophy
  B. Friedreich ataxia
  C. Corticobasal degeneration
  D. Normal pressure hydrocephalus

CONTEXT:
(no context)

Produce the JSON object.

response:
{
  "case_facts": [
    "54-year-old man with progressive gait difficulty over 3 months, now requiring wheelchair",
    "Urinary incontinence for 1 year",
    "Slurred speech and cognitive impairment (disoriented to time)",
    "Orthostatic hypotension (BP drop from 130/80 sitting to 110/65 standing)",
    "Mild tremor and rigidity in extremities",
    "Unable to perform repetitive rotary forearm movements (dysdiadochokinesia)"
  ],
  "task_type": "diagnosis",
  "decision_factors": [
    "Presence of autonomic instability (orthostatic hypotension, urinary incontinence)",
    "Presence of cerebellar signs (dysdiadochokinesia, gait ataxia)",
    "Parkinsonism features (tremor, rigidity)",
    "Rapid progression over months",
    "Cognitive involvement (disorientation)"
  ],
  "knowledge_slots": [
    "MSA typically presents with combination of autonomic failure, parkinsonism, and cerebellar signs",
    "Normal pressure hydrocephalus classically presents with gait difficulty, urinary incontinence, and cognitive impairment (triad), but usually without marked parkinsonism or orthostatic hypotension",
    "Corticobasal degeneration often presents with asymmetric parkinsonism and apraxia, but autonomic failure is uncommon",
    "Friedreich ataxia typically has onset in adolescence/young adulthood with sensory ataxia, often without parkinsonism or early incontinence"
  ],
  "candidate_considerations": [
    {
      "choice_key": "A",
      "relevant_if": ["Autonomic dysfunction (orthostatic hypotension, incontinence) combined with parkinsonism and cerebellar signs"],
      "less_relevant_if": ["Predominant cognitive decline without significant autonomic failure"]
    },
    {
      "choice_key": "B",
      "relevant_if": ["Sensory ataxia with onset in younger age, often with positive family history and pes cavus"],
      "less_relevant_if": ["Autonomic failure, parkinsonism, and later adult onset"]
    },
    {
      "choice_key": "C",
      "relevant_if": ["Marked asymmetry of parkinsonism, apraxia, and cortical sensory loss"],
      "less_relevant_if": ["Prominent autonomic dysfunction and rapid progression over months"]
    },
    {
      "choice_key": "D",
      "relevant_if": ["Full triad of gait difficulty, urinary incontinence, and cognitive decline without significant parkinsonism or autonomic instability"],
      "less_relevant_if": ["Presence of orthostatic hypotension and parkinsonism"]
    }
  ],
  "missing_information": [
    "Results of brain imaging (CT/MRI)",
    "History of falls or head trauma",
    "Family history of neurological disorders"
  ],
  "format_confidence": 0.95
}

QUESTION:
A 55-year-old man presents to the hospital with chief complaints of unintentional weight loss, anorexia, fever, and sweating. The patient has pleuritic chest pain, progressive dyspnea, and dry cough. There is no history of orthopnea or paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea. On examination, the patient is afebrile and pericardial friction rub is noted. ECG shows diffuse ST-segment elevation in V1-V4 along with T wave inversion. Chest X-ray and CT scan show anterior and inferior pericardial eggshell calcification. Echocardiography reveals thickened pericardium and signs of diastolic right ventricular collapse. Pericardial fluid is sent for Ziehl-Neelsen staining to detect acid-fast bacilli. Mycobacterium tuberculosis is detected by PCR. What is the most likely mechanism associated with the patient’s condition?

CHOICES:
  A. Metastatic calcifications
  B. Dystrophic calcification
  C. Secondary amyloidosis
  D. Age-related amyloidosis

CONTEXT:
(no context)

Produce the JSON object.

response:
{
  "case_facts": [
    "55-year-old man with weight loss, anorexia, fever, sweating",
    "Pleuritic chest pain, progressive dyspnea, dry cough",
    "No orthopnea or paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea",
    "Pericardial friction rub on exam",
    "ECG: diffuse ST elevation V1-V4 with T wave inversion",
    "Chest X-ray and CT: anterior and inferior pericardial eggshell calcification",
    "Echocardiography: thickened pericardium, diastolic right ventricular collapse",
    "Pericardial fluid: Ziehl-Neelsen stain positive for AFB, MTB detected by PCR"
  ],
  "task_type": "mechanism",
  "decision_factors": [
    "Type of calcification in chronic pericarditis due to tuberculosis",
    "Role of systemic vs. localized calcium metabolism",
    "Amyloidosis as alternative for pericardial thickening with systemic disease"
  ],
  "knowledge_slots": [
    "Dystrophic calcification occurs in damaged tissue without systemic calcium abnormality",
    "Metastatic calcification requires hypercalcemia or increased calcium-phosphate product",
    "Tuberculous pericarditis commonly leads to dystrophic calcification",
    "Secondary amyloidosis (AA) can complicate chronic infections but rarely causes pericardial calcification"
  ],
  "candidate_considerations": [
    {
      "choice_key": "A",
      "relevant_if": ["Hypercalcemia or elevated calcium-phosphate product is present", "Calcification is widespread in multiple organs"],
      "less_relevant_if": ["Normal serum calcium and phosphate", "Localized calcification at site of chronic inflammation"]
    },
    {
      "choice_key": "B",
      "relevant_if": ["Chronic inflammation with tissue necrosis", "Normal calcium and phosphate levels", "Localized calcification in pericardium"],
      "less_relevant_if": ["Systemic calcium metabolism disturbance", "Acute calcification without prior tissue damage"]
    },
    {
      "choice_key": "C",
      "relevant_if": ["Evidence of systemic AA amyloid deposition elsewhere", "Long-standing chronic infection with reactive amyloidosis"],
      "less_relevant_if": ["Imaging shows calcification rather than amorphous thickening", "No renal, hepatic, or other organ involvement"]
    },
    {
      "choice_key": "D",
      "relevant_if": ["Advanced age (over 70) with cardiac amyloid", "Restrictive cardiomyopathy without calcification"],
      "less_relevant_if": ["Patient is only 55 years old", "Imaging shows heavy calcification, not amyloid deposits"]
    }
  ],
  "missing_information": ["Serum calcium, phosphate, and PTH levels", "Systemic amyloidosis workup (e.g., serum amyloid A, biopsy)"],
  "format_confidence": 0.95
}

QUESTION:
A 67-year-old woman presents to a surgeon with a painless, slowly growing ulcer in the periauricular region for the last 2 months. On physical examination, there is an irregular-shaped ulcer, 2 cm x 1 cm in its dimensions, with irregular margins and crusting over the surface. The woman is a fair-skinned individual who loves to go sunbathing. There is no family history of malignancy. After a complete physical examination, the surgeon performs a biopsy of the lesion under local anesthesia and sends the tissue for histopathological examination. The pathologist confirms the diagnosis of squamous cell carcinoma of the skin. When she asks about the cause, the surgeon explains that there are many possible causes, but it is likely that she has developed squamous cell carcinoma on her face due to repeated exposure to ultraviolet rays from the sun, especially ultraviolet B (UVB) rays. If the surgeon’s opinion is correct, which of the following mechanisms is most likely involved in the pathogenesis of the condition?

CHOICES:
  A. Intrastrand cross-linking of thymidine residues in DNA
  B. Upregulation of expression of cyclin D2
  C. Activation of transcription factor NF-κB
  D. DNA damage caused by the formation of reactive oxygen species

CONTEXT:
(no context)

QUESTION:
A 71-year-old man presents to the primary care clinic with non-specific complaints of fatigue and malaise. His past medical history is significant for diabetes mellitus type II, hypertension, non-seminomatous testicular cancer, and hypercholesterolemia. He currently smokes 1 pack of cigarettes per day, drinks a glass of wine per day, and he currently denies any illicit drug use. His vital signs include: temperature, 36.7°C (98.0°F); blood pressure, 126/74 mm Hg; heart rate, 87/min; and respiratory rate, 17/min. On examination, his physician notices cervical and inguinal lymphadenopathy bilaterally, as well as splenomegaly. The patient comments that he has lost 18.1 kg (40 lb) over the past 6 months without a change in diet or exercise, which he was initially not concerned about. The physician orders a complete blood count and adds on flow cytometry. Based on his age and overall epidemiology, which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

CHOICES:
  A. Acute lymphocytic leukemia
  B. Acute myelogenous leukemia
  C. Chronic lymphocytic leukemia
  D. Hairy cell leukemia

CONTEXT:
(no context)

  "case_facts": [
    "71-year-old man with fatigue, malaise, weight loss",
    "Cervical and inguinal lymphadenopathy bilaterally",

    "Past medical history: diabetes, hypertension, testicular cancer, hypercholesterolemia",
    "Smoker, drinks alcohol daily, no illicit drugs",
    "Ordered CBC and flow cytometry"

    "AML incidence increases with age but often presents with cytopenias, not lymphadenopathy",
    "Hairy cell leukemia is rare, typically presents with pancytopenia and splenomegaly, lymphadenopathy is less prominent"
  ],
  "candidate_considerations": [
    {
      "choice_key": "A",
      "relevant_if": ["patient were younger, or had mediastinal mass, or rapid onset of symptoms"],
      "less_relevant_if": ["patient is elderly, and symptoms are chronic rather than acute"]
    },
    {
      "choice_key": "B",
      "relevant_if": ["patient had cytopenias, infection, or bleeding, or signs of myelodysplasia"],
      "less_relevant_if": ["prominent lymphadenopathy and splenomegaly without cytopenias"]
    },
    {
      "choice_key": "C",
      "relevant_if": ["older age, chronic systemic symptoms, lymphadenopathy, splenomegaly"],
      "less_relevant_if": ["if flow cytometry shows T-cell phenotype or lack of CD5 expression"]
    },

      "relevant_if": ["pancytopenia, marked splenomegaly, absent or minimal lymphadenopathy"],
      "less_relevant_if": ["significant lymphadenopathy present, and no monocytopenia or dry tap on aspirate"]
    }
  ],
  "missing_information": ["Complete blood count results (e.g., lymphocyte count, presence of cytopenias)", "Flow cytometry immunophenotyping results", "Duration and progression of lymphadenopathy"],
  "format_confidence": 0.95

QUESTION:
A 65-year-old man presents to the emergency department for sudden weakness. The patient states that he was at home enjoying his morning coffee when his symptoms began. He recently fell off a horse while horseback riding but claims to not have experienced any significant injuries. He typically drinks 5-7 drinks per day and his last drink was yesterday afternoon. His current medications include insulin, metformin, atorvastatin, lisinopril, albuterol, and fluoxetine. His temperature is 99.5°F (37.5°C), blood pressure is 177/118 mmHg, pulse is 120/min, respirations are 18/min, and oxygen saturation is 93% on room air. On physical exam, you note an elderly man who is mildly confused. Cardiopulmonary exam demonstrates bilateral expiratory wheezes and a systolic murmur along the right upper sternal border that radiates to the carotids. Neurological exam reveals cranial nerves II-XII as grossly intact with finger-nose exam mildly abnormal on the left and heel-shin exam within normal limits. The patient has 5/5 strength in his right arm and 3/5 strength in his left arm. The patient struggles to manipulate objects such as a pen with his left hand. The patient is given a dose of diazepam and started on IV fluids. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

CHOICES:
  A. Berry aneurysm rupture
  B. Bridging vein tear
  C. Hypertensive encephalopathy
  D. Lipohyalinosis

(no context)

    "Lipohyalinosis causes small vessel lacunar infarcts in subcortical areas, often associated with chronic hypertension and diabetes",
    "Berry aneurysm rupture causes subarachnoid hemorrhage with sudden severe headache, often with meningeal signs, not pure motor deficits",
    "Bridging vein tear leads to subdural hematoma, typically following head trauma, with slowly progressive symptoms"
  ],
  "candidate_considerations": [

QUESTION:

  B. The patient has a metabolic acidosis with hyperkalemia from increased total body potassium

A 45-year-old woman presents to the physician with a 6-month history of progressive shortness of breath. Pco2 48 mm Hg  (reference: 33–45 mm Hg)
Po2 63 mm Hg (reference: 75–105 mm Hg)
O2 saturation 91% (reference: 94–99%)
Which of the following would you expect to find in this patient?

CHOICES:
  A. Decreased lung residual volume
  B. Decreased diffusing capacity of the lungs for carbon monoxide (DLCO)
  C. Shift of the flow volume loop to the right
  D. Decreased lung compliance

(no context)

  ],
  "candidate_considerations": [

      "choice_key": "A",
      "relevant_if": ["Patient has predominantly restrictive lung disease (e.g., pulmonary fibrosis) or chest wall disorders"],

    },

QUESTION:
A 43-year-old man comes to the physician because of increasing shortness of breath for 1 month. He has been using two pillows at night but frequently wakes up feeling as if he is choking. Five months ago, he underwent surgery for creation of an arteriovenous fistula in his left upper arm. He has hypertension and chronic kidney disease due to reflux nephropathy. He receives hemodialysis three times a week. His current medications are enalapril, vitamin D3, erythropoietin, sevelamer, and atorvastatin. His temperature is 37.1°C (98.8°F), respirations are 22/min, pulse is 103/min and bounding, and blood pressure is 106/58 mm Hg. Examination of the lower extremities shows bilateral pitting pedal edema. There is jugular venous distention. A prominent thrill is heard over the brachiocephalic arteriovenous fistula. There are crackles heard at both lung bases. Cardiac examination shows an S3 gallop. The abdomen is soft and nontender. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

CHOICES:
  A. AV fistula aneurysm
  B. Dialysis disequilibrium syndrome
  C. Constrictive pericarditis
  D. High-output heart failure
